skill.name: writing-lib-functions
skill.description: Guides the creation of modular library functions for a Next.js application, with small files and clear separation.
skill.summary: Create modular Next.js library functions with small, focused files.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution at SKILL.md lines 3 and 8, but both are Markdown inline code spans around a directory name. The weak cryptography finding at SKILL.md line 3 is also a false positive; no cryptographic API, algorithm, command execution, network access, or prompt injection text is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 3 uses backticks as Markdown formatting around a directory path. This is descriptive text, not Ruby or shell command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is YAML frontmatter text and contains no executable code. The backticks only format the path name for display.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 8 uses backticks as Markdown formatting around a directory path. This is an instruction sentence, not an executable shell command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a Markdown bullet and contains no shell interpreter, command, or dynamic input. The backticks only mark inline code text.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 3 contains only the skill description and a formatted directory path. No weak cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic operation appears on this line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported line has no cryptographic API names or hashing logic. The static match appears to come from ordinary text in the description.

content.value_statement: Next.js projects can accumulate large utility files that are hard to test and maintain. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to create small, modular library functions.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of library functions for a Next.js application.
Limits each generated file to 50 lines when possible.
Splits related helpers into multiple files when practical.
Focuses work on the configured application library directory.
limitations: Does not define a testing strategy or test framework.
Does not specify TypeScript, JavaScript, or runtime conventions.
Does not include examples for error handling or validation.
References `srs/lib`, which may need confirmation in projects that use `src/lib`.

use_cases.title: Add Shared Helpers
use_cases.description: Create reusable helper functions without adding a large utility file.

use_cases.title: Refactor Existing Utilities
use_cases.description: Split a crowded library module into smaller focused files.

use_cases.title: Prepare App Logic for Review
use_cases.description: Organize helper logic before a pull request so reviewers can scan changes quickly.

prompt_templates.title: Create One Helper
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single library function.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a small Next.js library function for this task. Keep the file focused and under 50 lines when possible: [describe task].

prompt_templates.title: Split Related Helpers
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request with several related utilities.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write modular library functions for these related needs: [list needs]. Split them into separate files when that improves clarity.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Utility File
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced cleanup of an existing helper module.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this existing library utility into smaller modules. Preserve behavior, keep files concise, and explain the new file responsibilities.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Lib Module Set
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for a larger feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design the library function structure for this Next.js feature: [feature]. Propose files, responsibilities, and function boundaries before implementing.

output_examples.input: Create helpers for formatting note titles and dates.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes two focused helper files, one for title formatting and one for date formatting, with concise function responsibilities.

output_examples.input: Refactor a large shared utility file.
output_examples.output: The assistant separates unrelated helpers into smaller modules and explains which callers should import each function.

output_examples.input: Plan library functions for a search feature.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines focused modules for query normalization, filter handling, and result formatting before writing implementation files.

best_practices: Confirm the correct library directory before generating files.
Keep each helper focused on one responsibility.
Ask for existing project conventions before choosing exports or filenames.
anti_patterns: Do not place unrelated helpers in one large utility file.
Do not exceed the 50-line file target without a clear reason.
Do not invent project conventions when existing code can be checked.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps write small, modular library functions for a Next.js application.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill contains guidance text only and does not define scripts or command execution.

faq.question: Can it create multiple files?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends splitting functions into multiple files when that improves organization.

faq.question: Does it enforce tests?
faq.answer: No. It does not include testing rules, so users should request tests separately.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should users confirm first?
faq.answer: Users should confirm whether their project uses `srs/lib` or another directory such as `src/lib`.
